FINDINGS:
There is a large necrotic soft tissue mass in the medial aspect of the left
upper lobe measuring 78 x 57 mm in the axial plane.  This mass appears to have
its epicenter in the pulmonary parenchyma with suspected infiltration of the
adjacent mediastinum. The left upper lobe pulmonary artery is completely
attenuated by this mass. The left superior pulmonary vein is attenuated in the
area of this mass. There is loss of the fascial plane between the aortic arch
and this mass. Suspicious contiguous left hilar lymph node (5, 135).
Suspicious anterior mediastinal/prevascular lymph node (5, 115). Indeterminate
left lower paratracheal lymph nodes. The apical posterior segmental bronchus
of the left upper lobe (5, 129) is completely obliterated by this mass.
Nodular interstitial thickening in the apical segment of the left upper lobe
(5, 657) distal to the large mass may represent lymphatic and venous
congestion, but local tumor invasion (lymphangitic carcinomatosis) should be
considered in the differential. Multiple 2-3 mm part solid and solid nodules
(5, 44, 70, 71, 73, 101, 165, ___ in the left upper lobe, and lingula
as well as right upper and lower lobes are indeterminate and may be related to
smoking changes (scarring).

Mild centrilobular pulmonary emphysema. No pleural effusion.


Normal cardiac configuration. No pericardial effusion. No cardiomegaly. No
aortic valve calcification. Mild coronary artery calcification. No aneurysmal
dilatation of the ascending aorta. The pulmonary truncus is not dilated. No
pulmonary arterial filling defects to suggest pulmonary emboli.

Marked spondylotic changes of the thoracic spine with chronic wedge type
compression fractures of the mid thoracic vertebral bodies with resultant
thoracic kyphosis. No lytic/destructive bony lesions concerning for metastatic
disease.

No suspicious thyroid lesions.

Subcentimeter supraclavicular and axillary lymph nodes.

No gross breast lesions.

This study was not tailored to evaluate the subdiaphragmatic organs.

invasion of the left upper lobe pulmonary artery, vein and bronchus,
infiltrating the adjacent left upper mediastinum.

without obvious infiltration.

6. Small indeterminate pulmonary nodules in the left upper lobe, lingula as
well as right upper and lower lobes which may be related to scarring/smoking,
but early intrapulmonary metastatic nodules cannot be excluded with certainty.

A peripheral 1.3 cm x 0.9 cm spiculated nodule in the superior
segment of the right lower lobe corresponds to the observed chest radiograph
abnormality at the level of the right eighth posterior rib.  This immediately
abuts the lateral pleural surface, but there is no evidence of rib
destruction.  The lungs appear diffusely normal, with widespread centrilobular
emphysema, as well as more localized paraseptal emphysema at the lung apices.

ground-glass attenuation slightly more pronounced at the lung apices. Numerous
scattered small solid 1-3 mm diameter nodules are present in the right upper
lobe (images 67, 77, 80), left upper lobe (image 123), and left lower lobe
(image 106), all on series 4.  Additional 3-mm ground-glass nodules identified
in the left lower lobe laterally (185, 4), and superior segment (100, 4) and
on image 122 of series 4.  Several small solid nodules are also seen along the
minor fissure, each measuring less than 3 mm in diameter.  Mild bronchial
dilation is present within both lower lobes.

Numerous subcentimeter mediastinal lymph nodes are identified, but there are
no individual nodes measuring greater than 1 cm to meet size criteria by CT
for pathologic enlargement.  However, the number of nodes in the lower
paratracheal and precarinal area is greater than typical.  Heart size is
normal.  Coronary artery calcifications are present.  No pericardial or
pleural effusion is observed.

Exam was not tailored to evaluate the subdiaphragmatic region, but note is
made of a low-attenuation left adrenal lesion measuring negative 6 Hounsfield
units (image 240, series 4), suggestive of an adenoma.  Widespread vascular
calcifications are present in the lower abdominal aorta and the renal artery
branches.  Dependent gallstones are present within the gallbladder.  Exam was
not specifically tailored to evaluate the subdiaphragmatic region, and
assessment of the liver is particularly suboptimal in the absence of
intravenous contrast.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Spiculated peripheral 1.3-cm superior segment right lower lobe nodule,
highly suggestive of primary lung cancer.  Initial further evaluation with PET
CT may be helpful for further characterization and potential staging.

and are too small to characterize by PET.  These could potentially be due to
non-calcified granulomas, the possibility of metastatic disease or an
additional early lung cancer at one of these sites is not excluded, and
attention to these on a followup CT in three months is suggested.

disorder such as respiratory bronchiolitis.

4.  Low-attenuation left adrenal lesion suggestive of adenoma.

5.  Increased number of mediastinal nodes, none of which meet individual size
criteria for enlargement.  Further assessment at PET CT would be helpful.

Limited CT abdomen through the chest without contrast at the level of the
known lung nodule demonstrates a multilobulated 2.8 x 1.7 cm solid nodule,
previously demonstrated to be FDG avid.  There is emphysema, coronary artery
disease, and atherosclerosis.

Operators:  Dr. ___ attending, and Dr. ___
imaging fellow. 

Procedure: 

After informed consent was obtained from the patient, the patient was brought
to the CT interventional suite and placed in a prone position.  After an
approach was determined and the patient skin was marked with indelible ink, a
time-out procedure was performed per ___ protocol.  The access site was then
prepped and draped in usual sterile fashion.  The skin was anesthetized with
1% lidocaine for local anesthesia.  Under CT fluoroscopic guidance, a 17 gauge
introducer was placed into the lung nodule.  Two biopsy specimens were then
obtained once the tip was verified to be within the nodule with an 18 gauge
Bard biopsy device.  Fluoroscopic images at this time demonstrated no
pneumothorax or significant post biopsy hemorrhage.  The needle was withdrawn
and the access site cleaned and bandaged.  Post biopsy chest x-ray
demonstrated no pneumothorax.  After a period of observation the patient was
discharged home without complications.

IMPRESSION:
Successful CT-guided biopsy of a right lower lobe FDG avid mass without
complication.  Pathology is pending.
